speaker: Rob Moore
words: Here, Billy Kauder sings a song that was popular after Garfield's assassination. He tells of how and why he came west with his family in 1890 and talks about some of his neighbors. He also explains some of the difficulties that a homesteader could encounter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Have seen what? Well, have been around during quite a few different presidential assassinations?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Which ones do you remember?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know. I don't think I understand.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, do you remember when McKinley got assassinated?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes. It's awful.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I remember from the...

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh... Once that first presidential got... Lincoln was the first one that was shot.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, you weren't born yet when Lincoln was shot, were you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I wasn't born then, but I was the next one.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Gar...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Garfield?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, right. Garfield. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember that one?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah. James A. Garfield. There's a song, a song went.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was that now?

speaker: William Kauder
words: It must be my name at peace.

speaker: William Kauder
words: My name is Charles Guiteau. My name I'll never deny. For the murder of James A. Garfield I am condemned to die.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that song popular shortly after Garfield's murder?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did it take a long time? Where were you when Garfield was murdered?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We were living then in Missouri, in the northeast corner. Right, almost the most it was in Des Moines.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Before Missouri you lived in Illinois, didn't you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I was born in Illinois, yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where in Illinois?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Adams County, not very far from Quincy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your father know Abe Lincoln, or did he know him when he was an Illinois politician instead of a president? How did he know him?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, principally by his...

speaker: William Kauder
words: ...speeches, you know.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And... I think he seen him when Lincoln first called for the first 75 ,000 men. Oh, yes. He was a brother of father, younger a couple years than father. He went on that call for the first 75 ,000. Father didn't go, but he went. A brother.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your father end up fighting in the Civil War? Did he what? Did he fight in the Civil War?

speaker: William Kauder
words: But I forget who he was with now. He had a...

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know what you would call it, but it was about that long, the paper. The names, you know, who they... ...one country they went through, the companies they'd been taken, the rebels, and all them. That was all names on that. But we lost it. I don't know what become of it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was your father married before he went off to the Civil War?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: After he came back, he married your mother and they decided to move out west?

speaker: William Kauder
words: He married... I think it was in 1867,

speaker: William Kauder
words: I think.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I was born in the last month of the year,

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How old were you when your family made the move west?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How old I was?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. That was in 18... ...week of March in 1890, and I was just 21.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did you decide to move west?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Father had an awful poor heart. And the doctor told him that if he'd go west, he'd get among the pines, that he would live ten years longer. And we come in 1890, and he died in 1900.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, he had an awful weak heart.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there a lot of people coming west at that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh my gosh. There were trains, I think there was 18 cars, and they were all full.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take on the train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Immigrant train. We come on an immigrant ticket. There was a reduced rate.

speaker: William Kauder
words: It began on the 17th of March, on St. Patrick's Day. We bought our ticket the 17th day of March, and we come west.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take on the train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we left on Monday, that was St. Patrick's Day, it was on Monday that year, and we left, and I think it was... ...on Thursday, Wednesday or Thursday, we got into Walla Walla, and that's when we bought tickets for Walla Walla. We had a layover there at Pocatello, there was a train wreck, and the track was all tore up, and we had to wait until they fixed that, which took about all day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you get from Walla Walla to where you ended up on Cedar Creek? How was that? How did you get from Walla Walla to Cedar Creek?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we landed in Walla Walla about the 17th, about the 23rd or 24th of March, and I worked around there, the crops was all in, you couldn't work on a farm, but I did work for a farmer too, that is a summer farmer. Then we moved all up to Dayton, and Father and I both worked in the cell mill there at Dayton, up to Touchet, about 12 -14 miles.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Up the what? Up the what? You said up to something about 12 or 14?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Up the Touchet River. Oh. Yes, it runs through Dayton, and down, I don't know where it comes out of that wall, but I don't know whether it empties into the Snake or into the Columbia.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did you work there in that sawmill? We started in there, what time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Must have been in June, when we went to work there in that sawmill, and we worked there all fall, and that winter we cut saw logs and made cordwood and so on, and in the spring and about June. By the, I think in the spring we worked for that flume company.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The flume company?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Mmm hmm, the little flume that runs from the sawmill down to Dayton, that's about 15 miles, and we helped build that flume, and the flume lumber down in cordwood, made quite a lot of cordwood up there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much did you get paid for that sort of work?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We got $30 a month in the mill for working, and they paid us a dollar when the mill wasn't working, and we was making cordwood, we got a dollar a cord, and bought it ourselves. I forget, it was $30 in board when we worked in the mill,

speaker: William Kauder
words: or they allowed us so much when we boarded ourselves. And I forget how it was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: There was no union activity in the mills or anything in those days, was there? There was what? There was no union activity in the mills in those days, was there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I didn't hear of any, no.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We got, well it come out to about $1 .15 a day, it was $30 a month, and I'm bored, and we had to work 11 hours.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you pretty satisfied with that? How's that? Were you pretty satisfied with that?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we had to work with stars.

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, we wasn't satisfied with that, but we had to be.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When did you move up into the Cedar Creek country?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: 1891? And you took up a homestead then, didn't you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, my father took up a homestead right in the neighborhood, it wasn't much of a, it was a nice piece of land. It was a very nice little town, but it wasn't much, it was only about 40 acres. And then I took up 160 not far from that, but my gosh it was heavily timbered.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was the land like around there then? What was what? What was the land like then? The land?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Hope we got any in cultivation, it produced good. But it was a good job getting a change of cultivation. The big pines down there.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Gosh we cut down them pine trees, all with great tall trees. So I'm up in about 10 or 12 foot globes, roll them together and make a big pile of barnet.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a lot of underbrush in the forest in those days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: In some places there was an awful lot. It was pretty hard to work a tree, it was called a sarvis, you know. Sarvis berry.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how long would it take you to break in some land? How long would you have to work on your homestead place until it was pretty comfortable?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well we had, I guess four or five years before we got it so we could help ourselves pretty good. Of course we got small patches in, had pretty good gardens and raised all the vegetables.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it hard work proving up on a homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'll tell you it was.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'd never do it over again.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of things did you have to do to prove up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well we had to live five years on the place.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And we had to have so much in cultivation. I forget how that was.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I think it required a continuous residence but I ain't sure but I think it did. Okay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you build yourself a little cabin there on the land or what? Today, Bob?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. Yes, we built us a log house with three rooms in it, a living room and two bedrooms. Then we added on two more rooms, a kitchen and another bedroom.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you moved up to Cedar Creek, how did you know which land was open and which was already taken?

speaker: William Kauder
words: When we lived there in the canyon working at that sawmill there's another family that lived there by the name of Wells. And Mr. Wells and I went up in the fall before we heard so much about this Cavendish and Cedar Creek Country that we thought we'd come to local global. We did and we kind of picked out our homesteads.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: We didn't need to use a homestead locator?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the Wells end up moving down with you then too?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, Wells moved in. My question is on the marble brush patch.

speaker: William Kauder
words: He took up 160.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How come your father only took up 40?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, it happened to be right in the neighborhood, you know.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There wasn't much brush on it in the late night. It was in a nice shape, a nice piece of land. But it was kind of bottled up. Other places were taken all around us. But we happened to buy our way out.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The father being of such a weak heart, we didn't want to stick ourselves in too much of a brush patch.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Isn't it kind of a strange thing for a man with a weak heart to go out and start a homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: It took a lot of courage outside, yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why do you think most people were homesteading at that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, all the rims in Prairie Farm were all homesteads. Just like that reservation when it opened. Well, it's all homesteads.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The Nez Perce Reservation, the Camas Prairie Reservation, all opened for homesteads.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Why they wanted them all settled up by whites is a mystery to me. They'd better left that with the Indians.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would cause most people to want to be homesteaders?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would cause a person to want to be a homesteader?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why would a person want to leave the things they knew and go out and be a homesteader in a strange land?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, some for different reasons. Some on account of a change of climate, perhaps it's better for their health. Now like father, with that weak heart, of course, the change was better for him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did you choose to leave the home?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The home I had back east was worth the whole township of this country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The home you had back east was worth the whole township of this country?

speaker: William Kauder
words: It was for living and things. Yes, I could make living on it so much easier.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did you choose to leave then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was only the three of us in the family. There was father, myself and mother.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I couldn't go away and leave father in the condition he was in.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I stayed with both of them until they passed away. Never married until afterwards.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So you kind of helped your father prove up on his place too?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you build a house on your place also?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, I built a log house on that. There was no lumber around. I built a log house and a log barn. I cleared up some land on it. And then I didn't have it more than a year or two until father passed away. That left mother alone. I had to stay there with mother most of the time. So I sold off the homestead.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You sold off your homestead? How much did you get for it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Not very much.

speaker: William Kauder
words: It's about enough to prove up. If I wanted to prove up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did quite a few other people move into the Cedar Creek area at the same time you did?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Most of that country was taken up. There was a big settlement in there at the time we went in. And they kept proving up and mortgaging this JP Baldwin.

speaker: William Kauder
words: You heard of him?

speaker: William Kauder
words: He'd take mortgages on all those places for backing for his bank.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They'd go down to Lewiston, prove up and mortgage the place. And away they'd go.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They'd just leave?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The country was well settled when we first went in there. And five years later there were just a few of us settled in there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What happened to everybody else?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well they proved up, mortgaged and left. Didn't want to clear all that land.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well why would they homestead in the first place if they didn't want to clear all that land?

speaker: William Kauder
words: For a fortune. Just as hard as they could sell out for a big price.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And it didn't prove too good.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, your neighbors who stayed, what were they like?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was that? What were your neighbors like? The ones who stayed?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of people were they?

speaker: William Kauder
words: They were good people. Good neighbors.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They all come from different countries. Some from Texas, Arkansas, Illinois.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they all have different reasons for coming?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I suppose. I don't know. There were people they knew from all different nations. They'd gather there and they'd never ask one another any questions. They'd just leave it and that's for sure. It was none of my business what my neighbor was into before or what he'd done or how he lived or anything. They seemed to be all real nice.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well did you become friends with them later? Did you become friends with them later?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the names of your neighbors? On specific neighbors of yours?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, they're some of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Are you acquainted with Kendrick?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Some, yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know the Longs?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Uh huh. Well, they had a homestead up there.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Mr.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Long was a meat cutter, wasn't he?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Afterwards,

speaker: William Kauder
words: yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They were the oldest boys, wasn't he? When they were on Cedar Creek, was he a homesteader?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, he had a homestead there on Cedar Creek. Some of them boys were born there. There were four boys and I don't know, one or two of them were born there. I'll bring just more of them. That's the living room Kendrick now was born there. And maybe one just before him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did Long have a pretty nice homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, pretty good homestead.

speaker: William Kauder
words: He built a pretty good house on it. I guess it brought a pretty good price too at that time. Four or five thousand dollars.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The party that bought it last, I don't know how they got mixed up with the kids. They went to Kendrick's school and lived there at home and went down in the truck.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Some way or another the house got fire and burned out. It's a wreck now. It's funny there, there's three pretty good buildings that were burnt down up there in that country. On the Wells place there was a southern woodshed that was pretty good and the house was in pretty good shape. And this burnt. Then on the Kramer place there was an awful good frame house there with a basement on it. And this burnt. And then now this old place of Long's, this burnt. Well if you could have chosen for the timber you could see the places. Long's place and the Kramer place you could see one house and the other. The land cleared up that much land. But the Wells place was back where you couldn't see it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were most of the homesteaders single men or married men?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No married men.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did most of them bring their families with them?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. They take part in the left.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were they of different nationalities too? From different countries?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No I don't know. Oh yes I guess they were. Were Germans, Irish, English.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think most of the homesteaders were pretty religious people?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes Long's was quite religious. Wells was quite religious.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But there's a few in there like Thompson and House.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Some of those few others they wasn't very religious. We had church once in a while in the old school house.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would most people attend church whether they were religious or not or whether they belonged to a different kind of church or not?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well they might have belonged to different churches but they all attended whenever they could get a preacher in the school house. The neighbors would all turn out and help.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I had some at school.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There's nothing like that going on there now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember when Governor Steunberg was killed?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was people's reaction around here like?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well you couldn't get the sentiment of most of the people. They were pretty quiet about it. Some of them didn't believe in that the way Steunberg treated those miners.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Had them in the bullpen there. Of course it blows up that mine.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Wrecked the whole thing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think most people sympathize with the miners or with the mine owners during that trouble?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well they didn't express their thoughts very much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did you think about it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well the way I think about it is that nobody has a right to blow up somebody else's property. I don't believe in that. I have no right to go over to your place and blow your house to pieces or your barn or anything. I don't believe in that. I want somebody to blow up buildings.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't believe in that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you? What do you think about it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I don't believe in that either but my sympathies are kind of with the miners, with the workers rather than with the owners of the mines.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But not counting the blowing up of the mine.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You don't believe in that?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. I didn't believe it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you feel about the rest of what they were doing? The striking and trying to get better pay? How was that? How did you feel about the rest of what they were doing? The striking and trying to get better pay and things like that?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you feel about the trial of the men who were accused of killing Stuenenberg? Do you remember that trial?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't...

speaker: William Kauder
words: Let's see.

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I didn't think much of that trial. They had a couple of other men up for killing Stuenenberg besides George. And it seems like they got stuck on the old tree all together.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I forget what then. There were two others. One of them was Big Bill Haywood.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes, that's right.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How do you think most people, most of the early settlers and the home settlers felt about the law?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I never heard an expression.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how did you feel about it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, of course, the government has given the land away while we ought to comply with the law.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: It wasn't easy to have the laws enforced in that homestead country though, was it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, they had no trouble. Anybody else don't think that any contesting done. I think everybody complied pretty well with the law.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, like during prohibition, weren't there quite a few moonshiners up there on Cedar Creek?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, that prohibition. That was a fake.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was a fake?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There's lots of moonshine. I worked for a fella that was making moonshine. I told him, I said hide your thing and keep it away till we get through here. And then, do your moonshining. I didn't like it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You didn't like the moonshine?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why not?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't think it's right. It's against the law. People should abide by the law.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think that moonshine law was about the worst law they've had around here or have there been worse ones than that or ones that were broken more than that one was?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know how they didn't catch that just right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think that moonshine law was broken more than most laws or were there other laws that got broken as often as that one?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know about any other laws, but of course that moonshine was breaking the law alright.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would you do for fun when you had a little spare time and you thought you might want to go do something? What would you do in the early days around 1895, 1900, around in there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well,

speaker: William Kauder
words: all the spare time that we could gather up, we were burning out stumps or cleaning up the ground. I'll tell you, it was quite a job. Now we had one patch there of five acres that had a timothy. It was awful heavy. It was an awful, all the tims were way tall and heavy on the ground. We had to say that on account of the stumps we couldn't get no machine in there. You know that's very hard work. And then rake it up by hand. Even a small patch like five acres that was heavy enough to go about three tons an acre. All the stuff there had piles of shock at the shock.

speaker: William Kauder
words: We had awful good crops at those times.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of crops did you grow?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we grew wheat notes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, what would you do for fun? Would you go out and burn stumps for fun or do you have something else you do when you want to have a good time? Burn stumps?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We couldn't burn any only the pine seeds. They were picky enough to burn. And then the others we had. I got one of the neighbors to come over. I had five acres there that I cut off and I had it broke. And the stumps were a darn sick. And the neighbor, he was quite a man with this stump powder. I got a box of stump powder and got him to blow them out. He blowed out a bunch of stumps from me. Looked like I was clearing land again. My, I had a mess on the ground.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how many years of work would it take to bring a home set up to where it was pretty comfortable?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, that's different in different places. Some places where there were no redwoods or whitewoods or anything like that to fool with. You could burn them pines out and get quite a feeling in a short time. Now take us across the canyon from us over in what's called the Gold Hill Country. That is on that ground is principally more pine screw. And they just burned them pines out. And they had lots of clear land in short time. But we couldn't do that. We had too many firs.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And then first they rooted out first. Looked to me, huh, over an acre of ground.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take you to get your place pretty well fixed up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, it took quite a long time. I had to work out so much to make a living.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of work would you do when you worked out?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I worked out in harvest. ... machine.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Plenty of 30 days. And then sometimes in the spring of the year as soon as I got my crop in, I took my team and I worked down the road. I got $4 a day for that. I think I made $75 or $100 after I got the crop in, working on the road. And then I went out in harvest.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of work would you do on the road?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Grading. I had my team on the road. It was a grading principle part of the time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you follow the harvest around different parts of the county?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I went with a scratch machine and we'd get into a community and scratch that out and get into another community. That way I kind of got acquainted with the people and the country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What town did you usually go to for supplies?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, at first we had to go to Kendra, principally. And then Leland sprung up then and they had a good store there at Leland. Winters and Godsworth. They had a big store.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Winters and Godsworth?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you like Leland more than Kendrick?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, it was just handier. We didn't have no grade to pull when we come home. We'd go to Leland, we could buy just about as cheap as Leland as we could as Kendrick and we didn't have that grade to pull coming home. That time that grade from Cedar Creek down to Kendrick was a holy fright.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did many people have trouble on that grade?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Sometimes it took four horses to pull the wagon back up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How does it feel to be 105 years old?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Feels like you're worth nothing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Feels like what? Feels like what?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Feels like you're worthless. Better be dead. No.

speaker: William Kauder
words: You've lived through such a remarkable period of time.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I even got so important that I got a letter from the President.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Nixon. A personal letter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did he have to say?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, he congratulated me, you know, and talked about, if we had lots of citizens we'd have a good country. He made a nice, wrote me a nice letter. One of my friends has to read the letter to me. She keeps the letter. If I had the letter here, she'd let you read it. But I wouldn't know who, if they'd let it away from me, I wouldn't know who it was. I couldn't see it.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Tell you, when a person is blind, it's just the same as nothing. It's the most disagreeable thing I ever witnessed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What do you think stands out most in your life? What do you think has been the most important thing in your life over the past hundred years?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know. If there's anything that's one thing more important than another. I think that's all about the same.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When the changes were happening, you know, like the changes from the horse to the car and the car to, or the, you know, horse to steam and steam to car and all those sorts of things, did they seem like they happened real fast or did it seem like it happened pretty slow?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know how hard it is. In some ways, this changes. Maybe it's alright. But the way it's going now, I'm afraid they're going to have trouble.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Everything is getting so unmerciful high, unreasonable high.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I first landed on Caesar Creek, when Cleveland was president. You could buy a good cow for twelve dollars. Here, lastly, I heard some of them say they got a hundred and fifty dollars for a common cow.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Beef steak is out of sight. Pork and leprosy, I haven't had a bite of pork since I left Alma Betras. I recognize this pork.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What do you remember as being the happiest period of your life?

speaker: William Kauder
words: That's about when I was sixteen, seventeen, I guess.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why would you say that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, after that, I have to dig in. I work like a dick.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you glad that you moved west?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I'd rather remain for us.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was it like to farm?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I kind of liked to farm, and out west we had no chance for farming. Back east, we did. If I didn't have enough ground, I could run a few acres from the neighbor. Lots of times I rented thirty or forty acres from the neighbor for corn. That was the way it worked there. And the home place is drained.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was back in Missouri?
